stem: You are participating in the field rescue of an individual whom you suspect has sustained a cervical spine injury due to involvement in a motor vehicle accident. The injured individual is experiencing difficulty with spontaneous ventilation, and you plan to intubate. Which method of airway management/intubation is preferred for this individual?
rationale: The use of video-assisted laryngoscopy (VL) is recommended when placing an endotracheal tube (ETT) in patients who have suspected cervical spine injuries or other neck mobility concerns. VL is more gentle than use of direct laryngoscopy (DL); the use of a supraglottic airway (SGA) device would also be appropriate for this patient. Nasal intubation is infrequently utilized today although historically, it was considered the method-of-choice when airway management was needed for patients with cervical spine injuries.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a recently intubated trauma patient. The patient's vital signs are HR-104 sinus rhythm, BP-143/87, respirations-12 (ventilated), and SPO₂ of 100%. Which medication should you consider for post intubation management?
rationale: Ensuring adequate sedation for a patient post intubation is essential. Ketamine is the only answer choice that has analgesic properties and is a sedative. Etomidate is used as an induction agent for its quick onset; however, the medication's half-life is very short, making it less than ideal to maintain sedation. Also, Etomidate has no analgesic properties. While the patient may be unresponsive, they are still able to experience pain and discomfort. Succinylcholine and Rocuronium are both neuromuscular blocking agents and have no analgesic or anesthetic properties. In fact, there have been reported instances when patients are administered only NMBA and recall every part of the intubation procedure.

stem: A 49-year-old male who was elk hunting with his teenage son was accidentally shot at close range when his son was preparing to exit the hunting stand. All of the following internal organs are better able to tolerate temporary cavitation from gunshot wounds, except:
rationale: Penetrating injuries, such as firearm injuries or stab injuries, all involve the formation of a cavitation by the object that penetrates the tissues. A temporary cavity is formed by the transference of the kinetic energy from the object, in this case the bullet, to the tissues, causing a stretching of the soft tissues. A permanent cavity is formed from necrosing of the damaged tissue. Elastic tissues, such as the lungs, the bowel walls, and the muscles, are better able to tolerate this stretching force than are the more solid tissues, such as the liver and spleen.

stem: A patient who is being transported by medical air transport sustained an injury that requires splinting. In which of the following injuries is a traction splint indicated?
options: A possible fracture of the pelvis
An injury in which a wound and loss of bone continuity exist
rationale: Traction splints are rigid splints used to align and immobilize the bone to prevent further damage during movement and transportation. The traction splint immobilizes with a steady longitudinal traction pull exerted on the injured extremity. Traction splints are most commonly used for fractures of the femur. For these fractures, they may reduce pain and decrease the amount of bleeding in the soft tissues of the leg. Traction splints should not be used on an upper extremity injury due to the danger of further damage or impeding circulation. They are contraindicated with possible fracture of the pelvis, fractured knee, fracture or serious wound to the tibia/fibula, and severe injury in which a wound and loss of bone continuity exist.

stem: Using the Mechanism, Glasgow, Age, and Arterial Pressure (MGAP) scoring system, please assign a severity of traumatic injury score to a 64-year-old male patient who has sustained a blunt trauma injury to the chest, has a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 11, and has a blood pressure of 85/42 mm Hg, and identify at what level of risk this patient is based on the score.
rationale: The MGAP scoring system is used to aid in determining the severity of injuries sustained in traumatic accidents. It includes: Mechanism of injury (Blunt Trauma: +4, Penetrating Trauma: +0), GCS Score (+(patient GCS score)), Patient Age (\<60 years: +5, >60 years: +0), and Systolic Blood Pressure (>120 mmHg: +5, 60--120 mmHg: +3, \<60 mmHg: +0). The patient in this scenario was 64 years old (+0), sustained blunt trauma (+4), had a GCS of 11 (+11), and a systolic blood pressure of 85 (+3), giving an MGAP score of 18. Low risk category is 23 to 29 points (2.8% risk of mortality), intermediate risk category is 18 to 22 points (15% mortality), and high risk category is \<18 points (48% risk of mortality). The patient is placed in the intermediate risk category.

stem: While providing medical air transport to a patient who has sustained severe burns, you note the presence of significant edema as a result of third-spacing. Which of the following statements regarding third-spacing is most accurate?
options: Cardiac output is increased in patients with third-spacing
The edema associated with third-spacing results from fluid overload
Third-spacing is best treated by the administration of crystalloid solution
rationale: The phenomena referred to as "third-spacing" can be described as a translocation of the body's fluids from the intravascular space into the extravascular space (referred to as the "third space") as a result of injury (including burns), infection (sepsis), or pathology (such as liver disease, malnutrition, alcoholism, to name a few). Third-spacing often results in the formation of significant edema, particularly in the areas where fluid is being shifted from the intravascular bed into the extravascular spaces. While patients experiencing significant third-spacing may appear edematous, puffy, and fluid-overloaded, they are, in fact, hypovolemic and experiencing a decrease in cardiac output as a result. These patients are best treated with the administration of protein- or starch-containing intravenous fluids, such as albumin or hetastarch.

stem: A 16-year-old boy with a past medical history of insulin-dependent diabetes was an unrestrained passenger in motor vehicle accident. He has no known history of seizures but, en route to the ED, he develops tonic-clonic movement in all four limbs. On exam, he has a fingerstick glucose level of 77 mg/dL, a right-sided blown pupil, and a GCS of 8. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Seizure due to acute intraparenchymal bleed from TBI
answer: Seizure due to acute intraparenchymal bleed from TBI
rationale: Common etiologies of secondary seizures include head trauma, intracranial masses or hemorrhages, post cerebral vascular accident and scar tissue formation, infections such as meningitis or encephalitis, metabolic disturbances (i.e., glucose or electrolyte abnormalities), and drugs or toxins. Any symptoms associated with the seizure should be addressed to help direct workup and management. For example, a headache prior to the seizure is concerning for intracranial hemorrhage, while a fever and/or lethargy in a patient who presents with a seizure is worrisome for infectious causes. Pediatric patients are particularly susceptible to seizure following TBI (although it can also occur in adults). This patient was involved in a significant mechanism of injury, and the fixed and dilated pupil and GCS-8 suggest a head injury. While it is possible that hypoglycemia can cause seizures, it would be more likely if the glucose level was less than 40 mg/dL. Psychogenic non-epileptic seizure is extremely unlikely, given the lack of history. Cardiac arrhythmias are rarely associated with epilepsy and, additionally, this patient has no history of seizure.

stem: The Monroe-Kellie Doctrine is a principle of intracranial hemodynamics. It states that when any of the three factors increases, the volume of the other must decrease to keep the intracranial pressure (ICP) within normal range. Which of the following is not a component of this doctrine?
rationale: This principle states that cerebral blood flow is constant; there is a constant outflow of venous blood flow to make room for new incoming arterial blood, and the brain is enclosed in a rigid box. Under normal conditions, the volume of the brain (i.e., tissue itself) is 80%, blood 10%, and CSF 10%. When a condition changes volume in one of the three areas, the other areas are affected. These changes in the volume of one area and a decrease in another area result in increased ICP.

stem: A medical transport team is called to a field site in a busy urban area where two college-aged males were involved in a fight outside a bar, where you discover one victim writhing on the ground in pain. Witnesses state the injured victim was repeatedly punched in the face by the other male involved in the fight and, after falling to the ground, the other individual jumped on the victim's head and facial area before fleeing the scene. The patient is awake but in obvious pain, you note significant trauma and bleeding to the head and face areas, and when asked to open his eyes and look up, you observe that only the left eye is able to move as directed. Based on the information provided in the scenario, which of the following complications has the patient in this scenario most likely experienced?
rationale: Orbital blowout fractures most commonly occur in the pediatric-aged patient whose orbital bones are more likely to sustain a greenstick-type fracture than the adult; however, orbital blowout fractures often occur as a result of traumatic impact to the anterior orbit, such as with a closed fist. Causing serious complication to an orbital blowout fracture is the presence of an entrapped inferior rectus muscle, which can be identified by the absence of consensual movement when the patient is asked to look up. The muscle (or other orbital material, such as orbital fat) becomes entrapped in the fractured area when the traumatic blow to the orbital region causes a sudden, massive increase in the intraorbital pressure, pushing the orbital material into the fractured area. When the increase in orbital pressure is released (withdrawing of the closed fist), the sudden decrease in pressure results in the inferior rectus muscle becoming pinched or entrapped within the orbital rim fracture. Patients with entrapped inferior rectus muscle may also complain of diplopia. The presence of an entrapped inferior rectus muscle constitutes a surgical emergency.

stem: An 23-year-old male who was serving as pitcher during a non-competitive baseball game was struck in the head by a baseball hit from home plate by the batter. He experienced a brief loss of consciousness, and then quickly regained consciousness and appeared alert and oriented, and so returned to the game after sitting out for an inning. He subsequently began complaining of a severe headache, and when his teammates noted a change in his level of consciousness, they contacted emergency medical services (EMS). Upon your arrival, you observe the patient to appear weak, lethargic and confused, and note dilation of only one pupil. Which of the following etiologies is most likely contributing to the ocular symptoms described in this scenario?
options: Entrapment of the inferior rectus muscle as a result of an orbital blowout fracture
Hemorrhage into the intraventricular system from the subarachnoid space
Cerebral contusion of the temporal lobe
Compression of the third cranial nerve due to tentorial herniation
answer: Compression of the third cranial nerve due to tentorial herniation
rationale: Compression of the third cranial nerve often occurs in the secondary stage of epidural hematoma as a result of tentorial herniation of the temporal structures of the brain. Typically, the adult-aged patient who experiences a traumatic injury to the head which results in an acute epidural hematoma will experience a brief loss of consciousness, followed by a recovery period during which the patient is alert and oriented and appears normal and has normal neurologic findings. The patient's initial loss of consciousness, caused by the trauma, was caused by a tear of the middle meningeal artery, which subsequently experiences spasm and clots, stopping or significantly slowing the bleeding. Over the next several hours, the artery continues to bleed, causing the development of the hematoma, causing a subsequent increase in pressure in the epidural space, ultimately leading to stripping of the dura from the skull. As intracranial pressure (ICP) rises, the patient begins to experience neurologic symptoms again, typically headache and changes in the level of consciousness, due to the brain herniating through the tentorium in response to the increased ICP. Compression on the third cranial nerve results, causing ipsilateral dilation of the pupil, and the patient's level of consciousness and overall condition continues to rapidly worsen.

stem: Which of the following statements regarding epidural hematoma is most accurate?
options: Epidural hematomas are typically described as being crescent-shaped when seen on computed tomography (CT) scan
Epidural hematomas may be arterial or venous in origin, with the etiology of most being venous
Epidural hematomas in the pediatric population typically present with headache and drowsiness
Epidural hematomas that progress may cause compression of the third cranial nerve
answer: Epidural hematomas that progress may cause compression of the third cranial nerve
rationale: Epidural hematoma may be formed from a collection of either arterial or venous blood pooling between the skull and dura. Most epidural hematomas are arterial in origin, which are typically classified as acute, and have a lenticular shape (shaped like a lens) noted on radiologic examination. Subacute epidural hematomas more commonly arise from venous hemorrhage and are present for a longer period of time before symptoms are experienced by the patient. Ninety percent of subacute epidural hematomas are associated with linear skull fractures. The classic presentation of an acute epidural hematoma seen after the patient sustains the initial injury are a brief loss of consciousness followed by a period of recovery in which the patient is lucid and alert and the neurological examination is normal, followed some time later by the development of a headache and then a gradual decrease in the level of consciousness.

stem: Spinal cord injury (SCI) at the C7 level can be evaluated for by testing for motor strength through which of the following muscle actions?
options: Asking the patient to abduct her arm
Asking the patient to extend her forearm
Asking the patient to flex her forearm
Asking the patient to touch her smallest finger with the thumb of the same hand (thumb opposition)
answer: Asking the patient to extend her forearm
rationale: When the medical transport provider encounters a patient who has potentially experienced a spinal cord injury (SCI), the level of SCI can be evaluated for through the use of basic evaluation tools. The presence of motor strength loss can be evaluated by asking the patient to complete basic extremity movements. A C7 SCI can be assessed by asking the patient to extend her forearm; any weakness or difficulty encountered when attempting to extend the forearm, or any inability to extend the forearm, is indicative of a SCI at the C7 level. Abduction of the arm is used to evaluate for SCI at the C5 level. Flexion of the forearm is used to evaluate for SCI at the C5/C6 level. Thumb opposition is used to evaluate for SCI at the C8/T1 level.

stem: You are transporting a stroke patient status post hemicraniectomy. After a bolus of propofol for sedation, the patient's blood pressure (BP) is 102/55. The intracranial pressure (ICP) according to the patient's extraventricular drain (EVD) is 15. What is the patient's cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP), and is it adequate?
rationale: First, calculate mean arterial pressure (MAP): MAP = diastolic + 1/3 (systolic − diastolic). MAP = 55 + 1/3(102−55) = 55 + 1/3(47) = 55 + 15.7 = 71 (rounded up). Next, CPP = MAP − ICP. CPP = 71 − 15 = 56. Normal CPP is 50--90, but Brain Trauma Foundation recommends 60--70. So, a CPP of 56 is not adequate for this patient.

stem: You are the CFRN transporting a 23-year-old man who has suffered a gunshot wound to the right lower extremity and is bleeding from his femoral artery. His blood pressure is 75/30, and he is pale and short of breath. Which intervention should you take first to address the patient's hemorrhagic shock?
options: Apply firm pressure to the site of the bleed
Insert an IV and begin bolusing the patient with warmed crystalloids
rationale: The first step you must take is to control the patient's bleeding. A penetrating trauma that affects the femoral artery will not be stopped by pressure at the site of the injury alone. Pressure applied to the femoral artery by a tourniquet is necessary to control the bleeding. Once the bleeding is controlled, beginning the massive transfusion protocol and bolusing the patient with warmed crystalloids are appropriate interventions.

stem: A 19-year-old female who was a competitive gymnast was injured during training on the uneven bars. A medical air transport team was called to transport the patient after the responding medical ground crew became suspicious for a spinal cord injury (SCI). Which of the following SCIs has the patient in this scenario most likely experienced, if the air transport team noted loss of motor function of the patient's lower extremities, with preservation of her position, touch, and vibration senses?
rationale: Spinal cord injuries (SCI) are encountered most frequently in the age group between 15 to 24 years. Males are more likely to experience a SCI than are females, with excessive alcohol use and motor vehicle accidents contributing heavily to SCIs. Any patient suspected of having a SCI should be managed with caution and treated as if the spine is unstable, placing patients with suspected cervical spine injuries in rigid cervical collars and patients with suspected thoracic or lumbar SCIs on backboards. Level and severity of the SCI injury obviously varies, and patients may manifest with an array of spinal cord impairment findings. A patient who has experienced a flexion injury to the spinal cord may experience an incomplete SCI in which damage from bone fragments extends into the anterior column of the spinal cord, resulting in a loss of motor function (paraplegia) as well as temperature and pain perception below the level of injury, while function of the posterior spinal cord is preserved. The patient can appropriately appreciate light touch, vibration, and her position.

stem: Which of the following traumatic events is the most common cause of brain injury?
rationale: Falls from heights greater than 15 to 20 feet are associated with severe injuries and are the leading cause of traumatic brain injury (TBI). Motor vehicle crashes are the second most common cause of traumatic brain injury and the leading cause of traumatic deaths worldwide. While most TBIs are considered mild, their effects can be long-lasting. Depression, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), chronic traumatic encephalopathy, and personality changes are all linked to even mild forms of TBI.

stem: In regard to best practices during resuscitative care of an injured patient, to what does the acronym "FAST" refer?
options: method of resuscitative care provided during medical air transport and devised by the Federation Aviation Administration (FAA)
Provision of rapid airway, breathing, and circulation assessment by a select provider
The use of bedside sonography during resuscitative care
method of rapid sequence intubation (RSI) specific to provision of resuscitative care
answer: The use of bedside sonography during resuscitative care
rationale: The use of focused assessment with sonography (FAST) during provision of other methods of resuscitation has become almost standard during emergency care. FAST provides a means of portable, accurate beside sonography which is used to identify traumatic injuries which may not be as obvious to basic visual assessment. These cost-effective, portable sonography devices are now also being commonly used during transport missions to identify nontraumatic pathology which may prove life-threatening, such as aortic aneurysm or fine asystole. When used in trauma assessments, FAST technique has typically been used to identify free fluid collections (pleural, peritoneal, etc.), or sequela related to blunt or penetrating injuries.

stem: Which of the following most commonly used formulas for estimating the fluid needs of patients who have sustained burns recommends fluid replacement at a rate of 4 mL/kg/percentage of burn surface area (BSA)?
rationale: Fluid resuscitation in management of burns is a critical part of ensuring optimal circulation; however, the risks of both under-resuscitating or over-resuscitating these patients are great, necessitating careful calculation and administration of intravenous (IV) fluid replacement. Several formulas are in use for determining the fluid resuscitation needs of a specific burn patient, with the most commonly used formulas being the Parkland formula and the Modified Brooke (or simply, Brooke) formula. The Parkland formula recommends IV fluid replacement for both pediatric and adult burn patients at a rate of 4 mL/kg/percentage of the BSA, with administration of the first half of the total volume taking place within the first eight hours from the time of the burn. The remainder of the fluid is recommended to be administered over the subsequent 16 hour time period. The Modified Brooke formula recommends IV fluid replacement at a rate of 2 mL/kg/percentage of BSA, again, with the first half of the total volume being infused in the first eight hours following the burn injury, and the remainder of the fluid being administered over the following 16 hours.

stem: The medical transport team that was called to the field scene of a motor vehicle accident was required to transport a patient discovered to have sustained cardiac tamponade as a result of penetrating trauma to the chest. The patient is exhibiting early signs of cardiac tamponade, which should be treated immediately during transport with which of the following interventions?
rationale: When cardiac tamponade occurs as a result of trauma, it is most likely due to penetrating trauma. Blunt trauma to the chest that results in cardiac tamponade most often occurs as a result of rupture of the heart chambers and is typically fatal. While in instances of chronic disease contributing to the development of cardiac tamponade the heart may be able to sustain function without compromise even in the presence of up to a liter of fluid filling the pericardial sac, in traumatic causes of cardiac tamponade, a pericardial volume of as little as 150 mLs is enough to cause a deadly decrease in cardiac output. If the patient is noted to be exhibiting early signs of cardiac tamponade---pulsus paradoxus and tachycardia---the initial treatment should be with aggressive administration of intravenous fluids to maintain the systolic blood pressure between 90 mm Hg to 100 mm Hg, which prevents the blood volume within the ventricles from overcoming that of the volume within the pericardial sac.

stem: Which of the following trauma injury severity indexing tools used in the field to aid in determining severity of traumatic injury quantifies multisystem injuries?
options: The Mechanism, Glasgow, Age, and Arterial Pressure (MGAP)
rationale: The Injury Severity Score (ISS) makes use of the scoring of the Abbreviated Injury Scale (AIS) to quantify multisystem injuries sustained during trauma. These two scoring tools are the only tools that determine severity of injury taking into account injuries sustained across multiple body systems (the Trauma and Injury Severity Score includes the ISS score in its statistical equation). The ISS, like the AIS, scores injuries to the head and neck, face, chest, abdomen, extremities, and other external injuries, assigning them a severity score between 0 (no injury) to 6 (most severe injury and largely unsurvivable). Unlike the AIS, the ISS then squares the three highest scores, and adds these scores together to assign the injury severity score, with a range between 1 to 75. A score of 75 is considered to be nonsurvivable, and a score of 1 indicates only minor injury. If the injured party is assigned a score of 6 within any one of the body region systems injuries, the patient is automatically then assigned a total ISS score of 75.

stem: A 10-year-old child was retrieved from the field for transport after being struck by a car while riding a bicycle. The medical transport team's assessment of the child reveals a positive Kehr's sign with ecchymosis in the left upper quadrant (LUQ) of the abdomen. Which of the following organs is most likely injured?
rationale: The spleen is the most commonly injured organ from blunt trauma and penetrating trauma. A positive Kehr's sign (i.e., referred pain to the left shoulder during abdominal palpation) is indicative of splenic injury. The abdomen may be tender to palpation over the LUQ or may be distended and painful as well. Hepatic injury presents with right referred shoulder pain, not Kehr's sign. Renal injury presents with hematuria, abdominal pain, and flank hematomas. Pancreatic injury is not common in children.

stem: During medical air transport of a seven-year-old female who was injured in a fall, you note clear fluid leaking from her nose and suspect a skull fracture. Which finding would alert you that cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is present?
options: Fluid is clear and tests negative for glucose.
Fluid separates into concentric rings and tests positive for glucose.
Fluid is grossly bloody in appearance and has a pH of 6.
Fluid clumps on the dressing and has a pH of 7.
answer: Fluid separates into concentric rings and tests positive for glucose.
rationale: Leakage of CSF from the ears (CSF otorrhea) or nose (CSF rhinorrhea) may accompany basilar or temporal skull fractures. CSF can be distinguished from other body fluids because the drainage will separate into bloody and yellow concentric rings on the dressing material; this is referred to as a halo sign. In addition, the fluid will test positive for glucose.

stem: Your helicopter emergency medical services (HEMS) team is called to respond to the scene of a single-vehicle motor vehicle accident in which the driver of the vehicle was immediately killed after the vehicle became airborne while traveling at high speeds, and struck a tree. On your arrival to the field scene, you note the passenger of the vehicle being extricated from the vehicle by the medical ground crew, and you assist with placement of the patient on a backboard before loading him into the helicopter for transport. When assessing this patient's level of consciousness (LOC), the conscious state of the patient can be described by use of all of the following terms, except:
rationale: Changes in the level of consciousness (LOC) are the best indicators of change in the intracranial pressure (ICP), and can be best described by use of several terms used to indicate either a conscious or unconscious state. A conscious state can be described by use of the terms alert, lethargic, and obtunded, while patients in an unconscious state can be described as being stuporous or comatose. An alert patient can respond to his environment, including intervention from the medical transport provider, but may be confused, have slurred speech, and have noted motor deficits. The lethargic patient may appear to the medical transport provider to be asleep or drowsy, but can easily be awakened and should be able to respond somewhat appropriately to the transport provider. Patients who are described as being obtunded also appear sleepy or asleep, but are difficult to rouse and have difficulty responding verbally to the medical provider, and may appear to fall back to sleep even while being actively engaged with by the transport team. Stuporous patients may be in a light or deep state of stupor, with patients in states of light stupor moaning in response to pain or muttering one syllable words; these patients maintain the rudimentary ability to respond to a localized site of pain/injury.

stem: In which of the following patients is a traumatic diastatic skull fracture most likely to be seen?
rationale: Skull fractures are most often linear in nature and occur due to impact on the skull, most often extending from the point of impact towards the base of the skull. The impact may produce a single linear fracture, or it may produce a multiple linear fracture, referred to as a linear stellate fracture, with multiple fracture lines radiating out from the site of impact. A second type of skull fracture is that of the traumatic diastatic fracture, in which the skull sutures separate or widen as a result of a fracture along the suture line. This type of skull fracture most frequently occurs in the newborn or older infant population, but may on occasion be seen in an adult patient who has experienced significant trauma to the skull.

stem: Battle's sign is most often associated with a:
rationale: Signs and symptoms associated with basilar skull fractures include cerebrospinal fluid leak, mastoid ecchymosis (Battle's sign), periorbital ecchymoses (raccoon eyes), hemotympanum, vertigo, decreased hearing or deafness, and seventh nerve palsy. Periorbital and mastoid ecchymoses develop gradually over hours after an injury. The presence of a basilar skull fracture is a significant risk factor for intracranial injury. Skull fractures are usually categorized by location (basilar vs. skull convexity), pattern (linear, depressed, or comminuted), and whether they are open or closed. A linear skull fracture is a break in a cranial bone resembling a thin line, without splintering, depression, or distortion of bone. A depressed skull fracture is a break in a cranial bone (or "crushed" portion of skull) with depression of the bone in toward the brain. Diastatic fractures occur when the fracture line transverses one or more sutures of the skull causing a widening of the suture, typically seen in children and infants, but can rarely occur in adults.

stem: Which of the following is associated with Kehr's sign?
rationale: Kehr's sign is referred pain felt in the left shoulder. This pain is felt from blood filling the peritoneal cavity. Out of the answers provided, only a splenic rupture would cause significant amounts of blood in the peritoneum.

stem: You are called to a rural hospital for an 88-year-old female being transferred to a higher level of care facility. The patient was brought in by EMS to the ER due to suffering a fall. The patient reportedly fell last night and was unable to call for help. The patient laid on the floor for over 12 hours until her family members came home and found her. You package the patient and place her on the cardiac monitor. You note QRS widening and peaked T waves. What is the most likely cause?
rationale: Patients that fall and then are immobile on the floor, which is a hard surface, are prone to muscle breakdown causing rhabdomyolysis. This condition causes the release of intracellular contents including myoglobin and potassium. Potassium above normal limits will cause EKG changes, the first of which is a peaked T wave. As potassium levels continue to climb, the QRS will widen and eventually, if uncorrected, the EKG will look like a sinusoidal waveform. When the provider sees this, they should prepare for impending arrest and make every effort to lower potassium. Lactic acidosis would not cause the observed EKG changes. Troponin elevation from a myocardial infarction would present as ST segment elevation, not peaked T waves. While the patient could be dehydrated from being on the floor without access to water or food for so long, leading to high sodium (hypernatremia), this also would not cause the mentioned EKG changes.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient that has Battle sign and "raccoon eyes." What type of skull fracture does this patient most likely have?
rationale: Battle sign (Battle's sign) derives its name from Dr. William Henry Battle, an English surgeon, who initially described the ecchymosis in patients who had head injuries with fractures to the posterior aspect of the skull base, in the late 1800s. His description noted that to develop the sign, there was significant head trauma and may indicate significant internal injury to the brain and not just the posterior cranial vault or mastoid. The pooling of blood around the eyes (raccoon eyes) is most commonly associated with fractures of the anterior cranial fossa. This finding is typically not present during the initial evaluation and delays by 1 to 3 days. If bilateral, this finding is highly predictive of a basilar skull fracture. Classically, the tarsal plate will be spared. Further extravasation of the blood beyond the periorbital region is limited, owing to the orbital septum inserting into the tarsal plate. When a skull base fracture is present, the raccoon sign is present in 50 to 60% of cases. This sign can be easily identified and is usually associated with anterior skull base fractures, especially of the frontal bone with an associated epidural hematoma. Skull fractures are usually categorized by location (basilar vs. skull convexity), pattern (linear, depressed, or comminuted), and whether they are open or closed. Ominous examination findings include an abnormal GCS, raccoon eyes, Battle's sign, hemotympanum, cerebrospinal fluid rhinorrhea, and/or an abnormal pupil exam. Patients with acute cerebrospinal fluid leaks are at risk for meningitis.

stem: You are called to a scene for a 21-year-old male involved in a motorcycle accident; you determine the scene is safe. The patient was traveling at an unknown rate of speed when he ran off the road and collided with a tree. The patient is responsive to painful stimuli only. Initial assessment shows a patent airway, respirations at 8 and shallow. Breath sounds are absent on the left side, and you note tracheal deviation. The patient's pulses are rapid and thready, capillary refill is delayed more than 4 seconds. Which is the most appropriate intervention?
rationale: The patient is exhibiting signs and symptoms of tension pneumothorax. While the patient does need IV access and to be kept warm, the most appropriate intervention is to decompress the chest. We see in the example that there is tracheal deviation. This is a late sign and carries with it a poor prognosis. Decompression of the left side of the chest will most likely improve respirations and increase blood pressure. Depending on the patient's GCS, they may require securing the airway by intubation. However, the patient can have the airway maintained patent with an adjunct such as OPA if the patient tolerates or NPA if no facial trauma is present in the meantime, while the lifesaving intervention is performed.

stem: A patient presenting with muffled heart tones, narrowed pulse pressures, jugular vein distinction, and pulsus alternans on EKG would benefit from which procedure?
rationale: Patients experiencing these signs, also known as Beck's triad, are suffering from cardiac tamponade. The treatment of cardiac tamponade is pericardiocentesis, where a needle is inserted into the pericardium, the sac that surrounds the heart, and draining off excess fluid. This fluid is usually blood in the case of trauma patients. Needle decompression and chest tube placement is used to treat pneumothorax. The patient is not suffering from a disease process in which intubation is the definitive treatment.

stem: You are caring for a 28-year-old male who was involved in a motor vehicle accident 12 hours ago. The patient suffered broken ribs and a broken femur. The patient begins to show signs of respiratory distress, hypoxia, confusion, and tachycardia. You listen to the patient's lung sounds, and they are clear and equal bilaterally. What is the most likely cause for the patient's new onset of symptoms?
rationale: The patient has a history of recent trauma and long bone fracture. The patient is experiencing respiratory distress and has clear and equal lung sounds, thus ruling out hemo/pneumothorax. One of the risk factors with long bone fractures is a fat embolism. Cardiac tamponade would result in muffled heart sounds and jugular vein distention.

stem: You are the CFRN transporting a burn patient to the regional burn center. While en route, the patient's EKG begins to show tall peaked T waves. What is the possible cause?
rationale: Burn patients can experience electrolyte changes due to destruction of cells. Most commonly, intracellular electrolytes such as potassium are released causing hyperkalemia. Evidence of hyperkalemia is seen on the EKG first as tall peaked T waves. If the hyperkalemia is not corrected, the QRS will widen and, eventually, the rhythm will look sinusoidal. Hyponatremia generally does not reveal EKG changes. Hypothermia could produce an Osborn wave, not tall peaked T waves. Myocardial infarction would produce ST segment elevation.

stem: You are called out to the scene of an accident where a 22-year-old male fell from the roof of a fraternity house, landing on his head on concrete. While preparing the patient for medical air transport, you note the presence of Battle's sign. Based on the information provided in this scenario, which of the following types of skull fractures has this patient most likely sustained?
rationale: Basilar skull fractures are uncommon, but are most often seen in high-risk groups, such as younger males, due to their propensity to engage in risky activities. A basilar skull fracture typically occurs when blunt force trauma causes fracture of one or more of the bones comprising the base of the skull; often the blunt force injury pushes the mandibular condyles into the base of the skull, causing the fracture(s). Individuals who sustain basilar skull fracture will often present with Battle's sign, ecchymosis noted over the mastoid region of the skull, or will exhibit raccoon eyes as a result of blood migrating to the periorbital area. Other clinical examination findings include hemotympanum (presence of blood in the middle ear and/or ecchymosis of the tympanic membrane) and leakage of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) from the ears or nose (otorrhea or rhinorrhea). Individuals who have sustained a basilar skull fracture are also likely to have sustained facial fractures, fractures of the cervical spine, and intracranial hemorrhage.

stem: What is the first step in caring for a patient with a sucking chest wound?
options: Cover the wound with an occlusive dressing
Insertion of bilateral large bore IVs
answer: Cover the wound with an occlusive dressing
rationale: Upon finding a sucking chest wound, the CFRN should cover the wound with an occlusive dressing to prevent air from entering the pleural cavity. Most often, this is achieved by placing the gloved hand over the wound, then applying a commercial chest seal. Covering the wound falls under the breathing portion of the initial ABC assessment and supersedes circulatory intervention (IV therapy). While chest tube placement is the definitive treatment for pneumothorax, it is not the appropriate intervention to perform when doing the initial assessment. Needle decompression is only indicated if the patient has the wound covered and shows signs of developing a tension pneumothorax.

stem: Which electrolyte should the CFRN be most concerned with when treating a patient with rhabdomyolysis?
rationale: Rhabdomyolysis is the result of muscle breakdown or muscle damage. Potassium is the primary intracellular electrolyte and a large release of it can cause hyperkalemia. Hyperkalemia can produce life threatening arrhythmias. Sodium is the primary extracellular electrolyte and is not drastically affected by the breakdown of muscle fibers. Chloride is another extracellular electrolyte. Magnesium is also not drastically affected by muscle breakdown.

stem: You are called to a rural hospital for the transport of a 57-year-old male to a higher level of care facility. The patient was brought to the ER by EMS after a large, heavy piece of farm equipment fell and pinned him underneath. The patient's nurse reports that the patient was trapped for several hours before heavy rescue was able to free the patient. As the CFRN, which of the following conditions should you be concerned the patient may have?
rationale: After suffering entrapment, the CFRN should be aware of the possibility of patients developing rhabdomyolysis. This condition is caused when muscle tissue is crushed and damaged, releasing myoglobin which can cause acute renal failure. Damaged muscle cells also release potassium leading to hyperkalemia which, at high levels, can cause fatal arrhythmias. Disseminated intravascular coagulopathy (DIC) is a condition in which the patient forms many microclots resulting in the depletion of clotting factors. There are many causes of DIC, but usually it results as a complication of another disease process such as sepsis or Multi Organ Damage Syndrome (MODS). Sepsis would be the result of a bacterial infection. Pneumonia would result from either a viral or bacterial infection in the lungs.

stem: During transport of a motorcycle accident victim, your partner alerts you to fluid draining from the patient's ear. The patient has most likely sustained a:
rationale: Signs and symptoms associated with basilar skull fractures include cerebrospinal fluid leak, mastoid ecchymosis (Battle sign), periorbital ecchymoses (raccoon eyes), hemotympanum, vertigo, decreased hearing or deafness, and seventh nerve palsy. Cerebrospinal fluid leaks (otorrhea or rhinorrhea) are difficult to diagnose; however, the patient often complains of discharge of clear fluid from the nose or ears. Fluid may be collected and sent for analysis (identification of β-transferrin). The β2-transferrin isoform of transferrin is found only in cerebrospinal fluid, and not in blood, mucus, or tears. Patients with acute cerebrospinal fluid leaks are at risk for meningitis. Antibiotic prophylaxis is often recommended to reduce the incidence of infection. Administration of antibiotics should be done in consultation with the neurosurgeon who will be following the patient. If prophylactic antibiotics are instituted, the drugs selected should have broad coverage with good penetration into the meninges, such as ceftriaxone, 2 grams IV, and vancomycin, 1 gram IV.

stem: You are the CFRN called to a rural level IV trauma hospital for the transfer of a patient to the regional burn center. The patient is a 50-year-old male who became engulfed in flames while burning trash. The patient has circumferential burns to his torso and abdomen. The patient is sedated and intubated on a ventilator. En route to the burn center, the patient's ventilator starts to alarm high pressure and tidal volumes are decreased and pulse ox is declining. You listen to breath sounds and they are diminished bilaterally. What procedure should you perform on the patient?
options: Take the patient off the ventilator and press on the chest to relieve air trapping
rationale: Patients with circumferential burns to the chest are at risk of the skin and soft tissues not expanding and shrinking. This constricts the thoracic cavity and decreases the amount of air that can enter the lungs. An escharotomy is the procedure whereby pressure is relieved by cutting through the burned tissue to the healthy tissue beneath. This is sometimes referred to as a "chest box" and allows the chest wall to expand. Needle decompression and chest tube could be indicated if lung sounds were diminished on one side. However, given the patient's recent history, it is highly likely that the burn is constricting thoracic expansion. Taking the patient off the ventilator to relieve trapped air is a method to improve tidal volume if the provider suspects breath stacking or obstructive pulmonary diseases such as asthma. However, again, given the patient's history of burns, it is most likely he will benefit from escharotomy.

stem: Myoglobinuria may occur in a burn-injury patient due to which of the following?
rationale: Myoglobin (Mb) is a heme-containing protein, found in the myocytes of cardiac and skeletal muscle. Severe burns or muscle ischemia may result in rhabdomyolysis or hemolysis, leading to myoglobinuria which, in turn, may lead to acute tubular necrosis and acute kidney injury. Excessive fluid resuscitation may lead to compartment syndrome and subsequent myoglobinuria but, ultimately, it is due to the breakdown of muscle tissue and the release of myoglobin into the bloodstream.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient who sustained a chest wound from a gunshot. The patient is experiencing extreme dyspnea, tachycardia, and hypoxia. The chest wound is located on the right mid-axillary area of the chest. You note there is an unequal rise and fall of the chest with absent breath sounds on the right side. You also note a "sucking" sound when the patient inhales and exhales. Based on these assessment findings, what nursing intervention should you implement in this patient?
options: Place a sterile occlusive dressing over the chest wound and tape it on three sides
Place the patient in a supine position
Prepare the patient for a thoracentesis
Place a sterile non-occlusive dressing over the chest wound and tape it on all four sides
answer: Place a sterile occlusive dressing over the chest wound and tape it on three sides
rationale: This patient is experiencing an open pneumothorax, which occurs when an opening in the chest wall creates a passage between the outside air and intrapleural space. This allows for air to pass back and forth during inspiration and expiration, accumulating air inside the pleural space. Dyspnea, tachypnea, tachycardia, hyper-resonance and decreased or absent breath sounds on the injured side, and a "sucking" sound may be heard on inspiration (as the body shunts air through the opening in the chest wall instead of the trachea). In a prehospital setting, the wound should be covered with a sterile occlusive dressing taped on three sides (intentionally leaving one side untaped). This type of dressing will have a flutter valve effect; air can leave but cannot get back into the wound. Do not tape the dressing on all four sides; a tension pneumothorax can be created because air is being prevented from leaving the pleural space. If a tension pneumothorax develops, the dressing should be removed immediately, and a needle thoracostomy should be executed.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for an adult that was involved in a motor vehicle collision. The patient has rib fractures, left shoulder pain, hypotension, left clavicular and femur fractures. Based on the following signs and symptoms, where did the impact most likely happen?
rationale: We note that the patient has trauma to the left side to include left clavicular and femur fractures. The patient is also experiencing left shoulder pain and is hypotensive. We should think of Kehr's sign, or referred pain from blood in the peritoneal cavity caused by splenic rupture. The injuries are most consistent with a side-impact collision, specifically left (driver's side).

stem: A patient to be transferred from an outlying hospital by rotor wing has sustained a C2 to C3 fracture. Which of the following interventions should be considered before transport?
rationale: Evaluation of the patient with potential spinal injury should not differ substantially from that of any patient with multiple injuries, with the first priority being the airway. The higher the level of spinal injury, the more likely is the need for early airway intervention. For example, unstable spine lesions above C3 can cause immediate respiratory arrest, and lesions affecting C3--C5 can affect the phrenic nerve and diaphragm function. For this reason, some experts recommend that any patient with an injury at C5 or above should have the airway secured by endotracheal intubation. Delayed respiratory compromise can occur if spinal cord edema from more caudal lesions progresses rostrally to cause phrenic nerve paralysis. Inline traction is used to realign cervical vertebra that are malpositioned due to fracture or ligamentous injury, and should only be performed under the guidance of a neurosurgeon. Cervical spine stabilization restrains the patient from moving the cervical spine, and potentially causing additional injury. Two large-bore IV lines should be established in all trauma patients for fluid resuscitation and medication administration following ABCs. Insertion of an arterial line for blood pressure monitoring is case dependent.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient in flight who was involved in an MVA. The patient is exhibiting periorbital ecchymosis (raccoon eyes) and otorrhea. What condition should you suspect?
rationale: Basilar skull fractures are a type of traumatic head injury that involves a break in at least one of the bones at the base of the skull. They can occur when the mandibular condyles perforate into the base of the skull, but they most often result from the extension of fractures of the calvaria. Basilar fractures often produce Battle's sign (an oval-shaped bruise over the mastoid) or raccoon eyes (ecchymotic areas around the eyes). Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) rhinorrhea or otorrhea (ear drainage) may also be present. The "Halo" sign is the double ring pattern described when bloody fluid from the ear or nose containing CSF is dripped onto paper or linen.

stem: A 12-year-old female who is a member of a competitive wrestling team sustained a dislocation of her right posterior elbow after falling onto her outstretched hand during a tournament. The medical transport team is called to provide transportation for the girl to the medical center after it is noticed that her injured extremity appeared to be shortened and flexed and the girl is unable to extend her arm. The transport team completes the primary assessment and notes that the radial and ulnar pulses of her injured arm are diminished and capillary refill is 4 seconds. Based on the information provided in this scenario, for which of the following complications of a dislocated posterior elbow should the medical transport team be most concerned?
rationale: Traumatic orthopedic injuries on their own rarely result in the necessity for true emergency care; however, they may very well be complicated by significant blood loss or hemorrhage at the time of the trauma in the event of orthopedic fractures, or by potential long-term disability as a result of complications. Dislocation injuries may be termed as simple, complex, or compound, with simple dislocation involving the displacement of a bone from the joint. These injuries can often be treated by the use of short-term immobilization and manual maneuvers applied to move the bone back into correct alignment within the joint. Complex dislocation injuries include tearing of the associated ligaments, while compound dislocation refers to joint dislocation in which the soft tissue has been disrupted and the joint is exposed to the air. Joint dislocations may also be complicated by injury to adjacent tissues, requiring urgent intervention to prevent serious complications and long-term damage. A posterior elbow dislocation typically occurs after the individual falls onto the outstretched hand, causing the joint to become dislocated at the ulnohumeral and radiocapitellar articulations. The injured individual often presents with the injured arm appearing to be shortened and flexed, with the injured individual unable to extend the forearm. The injury may be complicated by compression of the brachial artery, in which case, the radial and ulnar pulses are diminished or absent, and capillary refill is delayed or sluggish. This complication requires urgent surgical intervention to restore the dislocated elbow into its natural position. A severe complication of a dislocated posterior elbow is referred to as the "terrible triad of elbow injury," and in addition to the dislocation, involves fracture of the radial head and the coronoid process. Ulnar and median nerve entrapment may also result as a complication of posterior elbow dislocation, and presents with symptoms of numbness and tingling in the forearm and fingers, and in severe cases, there can be loss of muscle strength.

stem: You are the CFRN called to a scene of a motor vehicle accident. The patient you find is a 51-year-old male who was the driver of one of the vehicles. Upon assessment, he is awake and alert, appears to be in significant pain, and is tachypneic. Upon auscultation of lung sounds, you hear shallow breath sounds and bowel sounds in the lower left base. Upon further examination, you find a scaphoid abdomen. What injury does the patient most likely have?
rationale: In patients that have suffered blunt trauma, such as in a car accident, the force generated can cause the diaphragm to tear and abdominal contents to enter the pleural space. This can be confirmed by the presence of bowel sounds while listening to the lungs. The bowels in the pleural space cause the lungs to not fully expand and, therefore, breath sounds are shallow and the patient is tachypneic. A scaphoid abdomen has a sunken-in appearance or is bowl-like. None of the other answer choices would produce these findings in a patient.

stem: You are called to a remote hospital for a 17-year-old male patient who was in a fight and brought to the emergency department after sustaining multiple wounds. The patient presented with hematuria, flank pain, and abdominal pain. Surgery is indicated. You suspect injury to which of the following organs?
rationale: These assessment findings are consistent with a renal injury. Hematuria is a marker for renal injury after blunt trauma. Because renal injuries are not usually immediately life-threatening, the transport team should give supportive care and identify the patient's risk for kidney injury when other life-threatening injuries are absent. At the receiving center, surgery may be indicated for major kidney injuries, urethral tears, or renal vascular damage. Many of these injuries, however, are managed without surgical intervention. Splenic injury leads to left shoulder pain, while patients with hepatic injury experience right shoulder pain. Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and elevated serum amylase are associated with pancreatic injury.

stem: In which of the following types of motor vehicle accidents (MVA) is it more difficult to predict the pattern of injuries which may be seen in the individuals involved in the accident?
rationale: Due to the predictability of the unchanging nature of Newton's Laws of Motion, we can accurately and consistently predict the types of injuries that may be incurred by vehicle occupants involved in motor vehicle accidents (MVAs). The restrained occupants of vehicles involved in head-on collisions will be most likely to incur injuries consistent with the forward force of their bodies (acceleration) being rapidly and forcefully acted against by seat belts and airbags (deceleration), while the unrestrained occupants of these vehicles are likely to become victim of injuries sustained as a result of Newton's Second Law of Motion, which discusses rate of acceleration being dependent upon the force and mass of the object in motion. The same can be said of rear-end collisions. Injuries sustained during rollover accidents are far more difficult to predict, not because of any failure of Newton's Laws in these accidents, but because of the tumbling and multiple impacts passengers of a rollover incident undergo.

stem: An 82-year-old male patient with dementia residing in a skilled nursing facility slipped while being assisted in the shower, resulting in him falling forward and striking his chin on the shower floor with his neck hyperextended. Emergency medical services were called to transport the patient and upon arrival, they noted a deep gash on the patient's chin, and the following neurologic findings: loss of motor control and weakness of the upper extremities coupled with complaint of pain and tingling to the hands, urinary retention, and priapism. Based on the information provided in the scenario, for which of the following spinal cord injuries (SCIs) should the transport team be most suspicious?
rationale: Central cord injury occurs most often in elderly patients who have fallen forward, striking their chin on the ground with the neck hyperextended. The injury is often frequently encountered in young males who have been involved in high-speed motor vehicle accidents. When central cord spinal injury occurs, the resulting disability takes place as a result of hyperextension of the cervical spine, versus as a result of cervical spine fracture or other disruption to the cervical spine area. Patients who sustain a central cord injury typically present with a greater degree of disability to the hands and upper extremities owing to impingement of the cervical spinal cord. The lower body and lower extremities are not affected as greatly as are the upper extremities due to the anatomical layout of nerve tracts of the lower body being located more laterally, while those of the upper body are located more medially. Bladder control is often affected, with urinary retention being most prevalent, and the presence of priapism may also be noted due to the dysfunction of the upper motor neurons. Treatment is typically with administration of high-dose corticosteroids, followed by physical and occupational therapy. Surgery is not usually warranted in central cord injury. Over time, patients typically regain lower function and experience improvement of upper extremity function, but may never recover full use and sensation of their hands.

stem: A burn wound is an injury caused by the interaction of an energy form and biological matter. Most burn wounds are what type?
rationale: A majority of burn injuries are thermal; thermal sources include flame burns, scalds, or contact with hot substances. Frostbite is often included in this category. Chemical injuries occur when the source of energy contacted is capable of causing tissue necrosis. Examples include strong acids and alkalis. Electrical burns occur when contact is made with a high-voltage current. Radiation injuries can be caused by both ionizing and nonionizing radiation; these types of injuries make up a small percentage of burns.

stem: Loss of motor function on the same side with vibratory sensation impairment on the opposite side in a patient who sustained a spinal cord injury is described as:
rationale: There are three major incomplete spinal cord syndromes identified by predictable physical examination findings: (1) Anterior cord (AC) syndrome, resulting from direct anterior cord compression or flexion of cervical spine, characterized by complete paralysis below the lesion with loss of pain and temperature sensation, with preservation of proprioception and vibratory function. Prognosis for AC syndrome is poor. (2) Central cord (CC) syndrome, resulting from hyperextension injuries, disruption of blood flow to the spinal cord, or cervical spinal stenosis, characterized by quadriparesis usually greater in the upper extremities than the lower extremities. The prognosis for CC syndrome is good. (3) Brown-Sequard Syndrome, resulting from a transverse hemisection of the spinal cord, or unilateral cord compression. Brown-Sequard syndrome is characterized by ipsilateral spastic paresis, loss of proprioception and vibratory sensation, and contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation. The prognosis is also good.

stem: A first-degree burn injury is more precisely described as a:
rationale: Superficial or first-degree burns involve only the epidermis skin layer, and may be painful, red and warm; the area turns white when touched, with no blisters and a moist appearance. Partial thickness or second-degree burns involve the epidermis and some portion of the dermis: superficial partial-thickness burns are usually painful, red, moist, with blisters and hair still intact; while deep partial-thickness burns may or may not be painful (nerve endings destroyed), may be moist or dry (sweat glands destroyed), and any hair is usually gone. Full-thickness or third-degree burns are the most severe and involve all layers of skin --- epidermis and dermis. Full-thickness burns are painless with no sensation to touch, skin is pearly white or charred, dry and may appear leathery.

stem: Diffuse axonal injury (DAI) typically results from:
rationale: Diffuse axonal injury (DAI) is a shearing or tearing of the long connective fibers of the brain as a result of shifting or twisting of the brain tissue. This most frequently occurs as a result of rapid acceleration-deceleration forces as is experienced in a front-end collision at high speed. In DAI, the brain is diffusely injured, most often resulting in coma, respiratory compromise, and decerebrate or decorticate posturing with severe traumatic brain injury (TBI), and in mild to moderate TBI, patients may experience symptoms ranging from headache to amnesia. Currently, there exists no treatment for DAI; solely supportive interventions are provided to injured patients.

stem: You are involved in the transport care of a burn patient with full-thickness injuries. The patient has been stabilized at the emergency department, and you are now transporting the patient to a burn center for appropriate management. A full-thickness burn may be characterized by which findings?
options: Dry and leathery wound surface, no pain to touch, marked tissue edema
Pink and erythematous wound surface, no pain, marked edema
Broken blisters, moist wound surface, pain, and minimal to moderate edema
Moist wound surface, no blisters, pain, minimal tissue edema
answer: Dry and leathery wound surface, no pain to touch, marked tissue edema
rationale: Full-thickness burns, formerly known as third-degree injuries, obliterate the entirety of the epidermis and dermis. These burns may damage the subcutaneous fat and may extend into the tendon, muscle, and bone. Full-thickness injuries are charred and leathery in appearance or white and waxy and lack pliability. Because of the damage to the vascular tissue, these wounds do not blanch or refill. Because of the loss of normal skin-related nerve tissue, they are insensate to touch and temperature. There can also be significant swelling associated with these wounds. These wounds necessitate skin grafting; scarring is expected with healing and frequently produces contractures.

stem: You are transporting a patient that exhibits cerebrospinal fluid leak, mastoid ecchymosis, hemotympanum, and vertigo. What is the most likely cause?
rationale: Basilar skull fractures are the most serious type of skull fracture, involving a break in the bone at the base of the skull. Signs and symptoms associated with basilar skull fractures include cerebrospinal fluid leak, mastoid ecchymosis (Battle's sign), periorbital ecchymoses (raccoon eyes), hemotympanum, vertigo, decreased hearing or deafness, and seventh nerve palsy. Basilar skull fractures commonly involve the petrous portion of the temporal bone, the external auditory canal, and/or the tympanic membrane. They are associated with dural tearing, which often leads to otorrhea or rhinorrhea. Do not place a nasogastric tube through the nares if cribriform plate fracture is suspected. Linear skull fractures are the most common type, in which there is a break in the bone but the bone does not move. Depressed skull fractures may require surgery to correct the deformity. Diastatic skull fractures occur along the suture lines in the skull, identified by widening of the normal suture lines.

stem: You are a CFRN caring for an adult patient with the following injuries: bilateral wrist injuries, calcaneus fractures, compression fractures to T12-L1. What do you suspect is the mechanism of injury?
rationale: Adults tend to land on their feet in falls greater than 15 ft. This causes calcaneus (heel) fractures and spinal compression fractures typically seen in the T12-L1 region. Patients usually fall forward after landing on the ground and reach out their hands, causing the wrist fractures.

stem: A long-standing feud between neighbors resulted in guns being fired and one of the neighbors sustaining a gunshot wound to the neck. Upon arrival of emergency medical services (EMS), the wounded individual is noted to be dyspneic and experiencing respiratory distress, and when responding to commands from the EMS team, his voice is noted to be hoarse. Hamman's crunch is also noted, as is air leakage from the neck wound. Based on the information provided in the scenario, which of the following life-threatening injuries is the patient most likely to have sustained?
rationale: Tracheobronchial disruption is an uncommon but potentially life-threatening injury which may occur as a result of penetrating or blunt force trauma. Most cases result from gunshot (or stab) wounds to the neck. Individuals who have sustained a tracheobronchial disruption most often experience symptoms of respiratory distress and dyspnea, and their voice is noted to be hoarse when asked to speak. Hamman's crunch, or subcutaneous emphysema, is also often noted, and air leakage may also be noted from the neck wound. These patients should not be intubated if at all possible or, if intubation is required, intubation should be undertaken with use of flexible bronchoscopy. While tension pneumothorax can be life-threatening and is often associated with injuries which cause tracheobronchial disruption, there is not enough information provided in the scenario to come to this conclusion. Esophageal injury may occur as a result of penetrating injury to the neck and also may be life-threatening; however, the patient's symptoms in this scenario are not consistent with an esophageal injury. Traumatic diaphragmatic hernia is not typically considered to be life-threatening.

stem: A 15-year-old male who sustained a spinal cord injury (SCI) after being stabbed while attempting to rob a convenience store was transported by medical air transport to a trauma center. The patient has undergone surgery, and his condition has been stabilized in the neuro-intensive care unit (ICU). He is exhibiting loss of motor control, touch sensation, and proprioception on his left side (which is where the knife wound was sustained), and a loss of pain and temperature sensation to his right side. Which of the following SCIs has the patient in this scenario most likely experienced?
rationale: Brown Sequard lesion (also referred to as Brown-Sequard syndrome) results from damage sustained to one side of the spinal cord only. This condition most often occurs as a result of spinal cord lesions or tumors, with traumatic events such as stabbing or shooting also contributing to the incidence of Brown Sequard lesion. Patients with Brown Sequard lesion will experience loss of motor function to the side of the body on which the trauma occurred, as well as loss of touch, proprioception, and vibration to the ipsilateral side. Loss of pain and temperature sensation is noted on the contralateral side. Treatment of Brown Sequard lesion is based on etiology.

stem: The medical flight team responds to a call to report to a local high school where there has been reported to have been an active shooter. Upon arrival they are directed to an adult victim who has sustained a gunshot wound to the chest, and during completion of the primary assessment, the team notes the presence of a sucking chest wound. Which of the following injuries has the patient most likely sustained, based on the information provided in the scenario?
rationale: A sucking chest wound may be encountered in patients who have sustained trauma to the chest, particularly gunshot wounds (or other penetrating trauma) to the chest. The transport provider may note the presence of bubbles of blood appearing over the site of a chest wound when the patient breathes out, or may hear the sound of air sucking in and out of the chest wound with the patient's breathing. While an open pneumothorax appears dramatic, it is less of a threat to the survival of the patient than is a tension pneumothorax, as the inspired air in a tension pneumothorax is unable to escape, increasing the thorax pressure, compromising both cardiac and pulmonary function. Historically, sucking chest wounds have been immediately treated with placement of a 3-sided occlusive dressing (often a petroleum gauze dressing) to allow for venting of the defect. More recently, commercially manufactured medical plastic chest shields (occlusive dressings) have been used to treat an open pneumothorax in hopes of preventing the development of a tension pneumothorax. And while this continues to be one of the mainstays of treatment of open pneumothorax, current research is looking at whether this practice should be continued based on animal study findings.

stem: A patient who is being transported by medical air transport sustained an injury that requires splinting. In which of the following injuries is a traction splint indicated?
options: An injury in which a wound and loss of bone continuity exist
rationale: Traction splints are rigid splints used to align and immobilize the bone to prevent further damage during movement and transportation. The traction splint immobilizes with a steady longitudinal traction pull exerted on the injured extremity. Traction splints are most commonly used for fractures of the femur. For these fractures, they may reduce pain and decrease the amount of bleeding in the soft tissues of the leg. Traction splints should not be used on an upper extremity injury due to the danger of further damage or impeding circulation. They are contraindicated with possible fracture of the pelvis, fractured knee, fracture or serious wound to the tibia/fibula, and severe injury in which a wound and loss of bone continuity exist.

stem: A 15-year-old male high school sophomore who was a member of his school's football team sustained a spinal cord injury (SCI) when he inadvertently put his head down while running and drove the crown of his head into the shoulder of the ball carrier during a tackle. When the medical transport arrives, the patient has been placed on a backboard by the field team and is already intubated due to the inability to breathe on his own. The ground team reports the patient had been alert and was attempting to speak at the time of their arrival, and was noted to be able to turn his head. Primary assessment by the air transport team reveals full loss of motor and sensory function of upper and lower extremities. Based on the information provided in this scenario, which of the following levels of SCI is this patient most likely to have experienced?
rationale: Pediatric-aged patients between the ages of 8 to 15 years are at greatest risk of spinal cord injury (SCI) with subaxial vertebral body fractures affecting the C3 to C7 level of the spine as a result of injuries sustained during sports activities, falls, diving accidents, and motor vehicle crashes. A SCI to the C3-C4 level occurs during trauma when the head is bent forward and the spinal cord is compressed at great force when the head is driven into an object in front of it. Fracture of the vertebral body is typically followed by edema of the spinal cord, resulting in life-threatening disability. A C3-C4 SCI may result in the injured patient being unable to breathe on his own, and with full loss of motor and sensory functions of both his upper and lower extremities, but with the ability to move his head and speak. The C3-C4 spinal cord injured patient will most likely require full care with all activities and functions of daily life for the rest of his life. A C1-C2 SCI, also referred to as an atlantoaxial injury, is the most serious of the SCIs, and may result in immediate death. A C5-C6 SCI may result in full loss of function of the lower extremities, and limited function of the upper extremities, with likely paralysis of some portion of the trunk, as well as the hands and wrists. Breathing may be affected, but the patient should be able to sustain breathing on his own. The patient will have no control of his bowel and bladder function. A C7-C8 SCI may result in retention of some arm function and the ability to move the shoulders, as well as limited hand mobility. There is full loss of motor and sensory function of the lower extremities, and bowel and bladder function is impaired.

stem: Which type of burn causes saponification?
rationale: Alkali burns are caused by basic chemicals such as lye. These burns liquify the fatty tissues of the skin. Treatment includes dilution with copious amounts of water to neutralize the basic pH. Acidic burns are treated the same way, however, they cause coagulation necrosis. AC current causes explosive exit wounds while DC current exit wounds are discreet.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient with a traumatic brain injury. The patient report states that the patient came to the ER with initial transient loss of consciousness and then had a period of lucidity followed by decreasing level of consciousness again. What type of hemorrhage is the patient suffering from?
rationale: An epidural hematoma results when blood collects in the potential space between skull and dura mater, typically as a result of blunt trauma to the temporal or temporal-parietal area. This, associated with a skull fracture and damage to the middle meningeal artery, is the primary mechanism of injury. Occasionally, epidural hematoma may result from trauma to the parietal-occipital region or the posterior fossa, which causes a tear within the venous sinuses. A classic description of an epidural hematoma is a significant blunt trauma to the head, with loss of consciousness or altered sensorium, followed by a lucid period and subsequent rapid neurologic demise; a minority of cases actually presents this way. A traumatic blow to the temporal bone at the lateral aspect of the head carries the highest risk (e.g., baseball or pool stick injury).

stem: The primary task in managing a burn-injury patient is to:
options: Dress the injury with saline-soaked gauze
rationale: When managing a burn-injury patient, the most important step is to stop the burning process, whether by removing from an ignition source or diluting/removing a chemical source.

stem: You are transporting a trauma patient who fell from a three-story building and suffered blunt injury and, during transport, the patient begins exhibiting Beck's triad. What are the classic signs of Beck's triad, and of what underlying pathology are they indicative?
options: Sudden onset of interscapular pain, anterior chest pain, and weak peripheral pulses; aortic dissection
Hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy; hypovolemic shock
Distended neck veins, decreased blood pressure, and muffled heart sounds; cardiac tamponade
Pulsus paradoxus, tachycardia, and a friction rub; cardiac effusion
answer: Distended neck veins, decreased blood pressure, and muffled heart sounds; cardiac tamponade
rationale: Patients who have sustained blunt trauma or penetrating injuries may develop cardiac tamponade as a result of the rapid accumulation of fluid in the pericardium. Volume in the amount of 150 to 200 mL may rapidly accumulate in the space designed to hold only 10 to 20 mLs of fluid, resulting in the tamponade. The signs of Beck's triad, which include distention of the neck veins, a decrease in systemic blood pressure, and muffled heart sounds, are classic in helping to diagnose cardiac tamponade. Other signs may include the development of pulsus paradoxus and tachycardia, as well as ECG changes. The "triad of death," hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy, often results from hypovolemic shock as a result of hemorrhage. Patients experiencing non-traumatic aortic dissection often describe the sudden onset of a "ripping" interscapular pain, anterior chest pain (which may extend down the legs), and may have weak or absent peripheral pulses.

stem: Which of the following types of subdural hematoma has the highest association with high morbidity and mortality?
rationale: Subdural hematoma, which is a collection of blood located between the arachnoid and dura maters typically as a result of trauma to the head, are classified as acute, subacute, and chronic. The classification is made based upon the timing of when signs and symptoms of the intracranial hemorrhage become apparent. Acute subdural hematoma symptoms present within 24 hours of the initial head trauma, and have highest association with death and disability. The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS), used to aid in determining level of neurologic injury, can be used to score patients with subdural hematoma; a GCS score of less than 8 has an associated mortality rate of 50% to 90%. The high mortality rates associated with subdural hematoma are due to both the severity of the injuries associated with the development of the hematoma, as well as with the rapid, significant increase in intracranial pressure (ICP) experienced with subdural hematoma. Symptoms of a subacute subdural hematoma manifest within 2 to 10 days of the initial injury, and symptoms of chronic subdural hematoma do not manifest until at least 2 weeks after the injury. The epidural hematoma can be described as being lenticular in shape.

stem: What is a hallmark sign or symptom of compartment syndrome?
options: The affected extremity distal to the site of injury feels cooler to the touch compared to the unaffected extremity
The affected extremity appears pale or dusky and has a capillary refill of greater than 2 seconds
Extreme pain with passive touch or movement of the affected extremity
answer: Extreme pain with passive touch or movement of the affected extremity
rationale: Compartment syndrome develops when too much pressure is exerted within the muscle compartments of an extremity or other body area. This can happen when there is bleeding, soft tissue swelling (e.g., bone fracture), fluid accumulation (e.g., burn injury), or pressure from external sources such as splinting. As the pressure builds within the compartment, this will cut off blood supply and nerve function to the muscle/area. If left uncorrected for 6 hours or more, the damage is permanent. Patients often describe compartment syndrome pain as deep, burning, or unrelenting. Pain that is worst with passive touch or movement, elevating the limb, or any pressure is a hallmark symptom. Pain medications will not generally relieve pain caused by compartment syndrome. The other choices (describing poikilothermia, pulselessness, and pallor) are clinical signs of this condition.

stem: A 79-year-old female who was struck by a car while crossing the street sustained what appeared to be mostly minor injuries in addition to four fractured ribs on her right rib cage and a fracture of her right clavicle. The patient was transported to the nearest hospital for treatment and observation, and a chest radiograph obtained in the emergency department revealed a right hemopneumothorax; all other imaging studies were negative.
